edges.description: The expanded non-coding CGG repeat is the substrate for repeat-associated non-AUG (RAN) translation into a toxic polyglycine protein.
edges.target: RAN translation of toxic polyglycine protein

edges.description: The aggregation-prone RAN-translated polyglycine protein (and expanded repeat RNA) drives formation of p62/ubiquitin-positive intranuclear inclusions.
edges.source: RAN translation of toxic polyglycine protein

edges.description: Accumulating intranuclear inclusions and proteotoxic/RNA-mediated injury cause progressive degeneration of skeletal muscle fibers.
Progressive extraocular/levator muscle degeneration produces ptosis.
Extraocular muscle involvement produces progressive external ophthalmoplegia.
Facial muscle degeneration produces facial weakness.
Distal limb muscle degeneration produces distal-predominant weakness.
Respiratory muscle degeneration produces ventilatory insufficiency in advanced disease.
edges.target: Respiratory insufficiency due to muscle weakness

nodes.description: Distal limb weakness, often beginning in the lower legs, is a defining feature.

evidence.snippet: Limb muscle weakness was predominantly distal in 53 of 64 patients (83%)
weakness of the masseter, facial, pharyngeal, and distal limb muscles.

nodes.description: Weakness of the facial musculature contributes to the characteristic facies.

evidence.snippet: weakness of the masseter, facial, pharyngeal, and distal limb muscles.
we identified an abnormal GGC repeat expansion in the 5' UTR of GIPC1

nodes.description: The RAN-translated polyglycine protein and/or expanded repeat RNA drives formation of eosinophilic, p62- and ubiquitin-positive intranuclear inclusions in skeletal muscle fibers and, in NOTCH2NLC-related disease, in neurons and other tissues.

evidence.snippet: anti-poly-ubiquitinated proteins, anti-SUMO1 and anti-p62 antibodies
co-localizes with p62 in intranuclear inclusions.
oculopharyngodistal myopathy, in LOC642361/NUTM2B-AS1 and LRP12, respectively.
Oculopharyngodistal myopathy (OPDM) is a rare hereditary muscle disease
noncoding CGG repeat expansions in NBPF19 (NOTCH2NLC) as the causative mutations

nodes.description: OPDM is caused by abnormally expanded non-coding CGG trinucleotide repeats in the 5' untranslated/regulatory regions of LRP12 (OPDM1), GIPC1 (OPDM2), NOTCH2NLC (OPDM3), or RILPL1 (OPDM4). Repeat length above a gene-specific threshold confers pathogenicity.

evidence.snippet: Recent studies indicate that CGG repeat expansions in LRP12, GIPC1, and NOTCH2NLC are associated with oculopharyngodistal myopathy (OPDM) types 1, 2, and 3, respectively.
Intriguingly, all four types of OPDM have been found to be associated with the CGG repeat expansions located in 5' UTRs.

nodes.description: External ophthalmoplegia with limited eye movements is characteristic.

evidence.snippet: characterized by progressive ptosis, external ophthalmoplegia, and

nodes.description: Accumulating proteotoxic and RNA-mediated injury leads to degeneration of extraocular, facial, bulbar, respiratory, and distal limb muscles, with myopathic changes and rimmed vacuoles on biopsy and progressive weakness.
Progressive drooping of the eyelids is a hallmark early feature of OPDM.

Ptosis was observed in 62 of 64 patients

nodes.description: The expanded CGG repeat undergoes repeat-associated non-AUG (RAN) translation, producing a toxic polyglycine-containing protein. This cap-independent, AUG-independent translation generates aggregation-prone polypeptides that accumulate in affected cells.
nodes.id: RAN translation of toxic polyglycine protein

evidence.snippet: expanded CGG repeat might be translated into a toxic poly-glycine protein that co-localizes with p62 in intranuclear inclusions.
the toxic RNA gain-of-function effects also contributed to the pathogenesis
a CGG repeat expansion in the 5' UTR of RILPL1 is associated with familial and simplex OPDM type 4 (OPDM4).

nodes.description: Respiratory muscle involvement can lead to respiratory insufficiency.
nodes.id: Respiratory insufficiency due to muscle weakness
